title: Paths to Matroid Representations
speaker: Tong Jin
abstract: In this talk, I will first review previous works on matroid representations initiated by Tutte in 1958, generalized by Dress and Wenzel in the 1980s, and refined by Baker, Bowler, and Lorscheid more recently using the language of F1-algebra. It turns out that one of the key ingredients of a "universal" proof of many representation theorems is a connectivity (or homotopy) statement of the lattice of flats of a matroid that confirms the existence of a certain path of hyperplanes. Next, I will extend this theory to orthogonal matroids. Orthogonal matroids appear in many natural ways to generalize a matroid, such as type D_n Coxeter matroids or tight 2-matroids. I will also describe the "lattice" of an orthogonal matroid and give a connectivity statement for the orthogonal matroid that produces as well many nice theorems of their representations.

proof_stages.label: Regular matroids and Tutte’s excluded-minor theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk opens by defining regular matroids as matroids representable over all fields and recalls Tutte’s theorem characterizing them by excluding U_{2,4}, the Fano matroid, and the dual Fano matroid as minors.

proof_stages.label: Circuit-vector representation via modular triples
proof_stages.summary: Tutte’s path-proof idea is reformulated: instead of row spaces, one can encode a representation by circuit vectors satisfying linear-dependence conditions on modular triples, a viewpoint generalized by Dress and Wenzel.

proof_stages.label: Path theorem and cross-ratio generators
proof_stages.summary: Tutte’s path theorem connects circuits through modular pairs, and the associated cross ratios are invariant under row operations and column rescaling and determine representations modulo column scaling, equivalently generating the foundation.

proof_stages.label: Cycle decomposition and relations among cross ratios
proof_stages.summary: Different circuit paths give consistency conditions, and Tutte’s hard decomposition of large cycles into elementary small cycles explains the relations among cross ratios and the appearance of small excluded minors.

proof_stages.label: Orthogonal matroids and their combinatorial data
proof_stages.summary: The main objects, orthogonal matroids of type D_n, are introduced via realizations by skew-symmetric matrices, starred ground sets, transversals, bases, independent sets, and circuits.

proof_stages.label: Rank, modularity, and circuit-vector representations for orthogonal matroids
proof_stages.summary: The rank function is extended to non-admissible subsets, modular pairs and triples of circuits are defined, a circuit-vector representation theorem for orthogonal matroids is cited, and the resulting foundations are noted to lack known generators and relations.

proof_stages.label: Failure of the naive orthogonal path theorem
proof_stages.summary: A connected-looking orthogonal matroid shows that the ordinary path theorem cannot be copied verbatim, because circuits may split into separated families after contraction.

proof_stages.label: Irreducibility and the orthogonal path conjecture
proof_stages.summary: Irreducibility is proposed as the right replacement for connectedness, leading to a conjectural orthogonal path theorem whose cross-ratio generators should again come from small minors; the questions discuss minor lists, checked small cases, and regular orthogonal matroids.

examples.engagement_reason: The speaker represents U_{2,6} by a specific matrix, computes circuit vectors and their dependencies, and runs paths/cross-ratio constructions on its circuits.

examples.name: Five-element orthogonal-matroid obstruction
examples.note: This constructed orthogonal matroid has two circuits that cannot be joined by the desired circuit path, forcing the replacement of ordinary connectedness by irreducibility.
